oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Parrots: Invasive Species or Colorful Colonizers
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Marshall, Tom
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: This item originally appeared in AFA Watchbird Volume 49 Number 2 (July 2022).The following is an excerpt from the full PDF link.Parrots: Invasive Species or Colorful ColonizersCertainly, the pet trade, along with habitat loss, have been important factors in the decline of many varied species of parrot populations. However, the pet trade has also been closely associated primarily with the inadvertent introduction and colonization of parrots in strikingly new geographical regions and habitats. If the habitats are suitable, while possibly being as different as rural vs. urban, parrots with adaptable traits, a defining characteristic of this avian genre, have been remarkably successful in new and novel environments. Given their behavioral flexibility, and large brains, that rival those of some primates, and their sociability, parrots often become naturalized avian citizens to the delight of much of the human population, their neighbors.According to CITES’ data on legally traded parrots, which started being collected and analyzed in 1975, up to and through the year, 2015, there were 19,607,897 of 336 different species of parrots legally imported from the wild. Of that figure, only four species represented over 60% of the total trade in the period, 1975-2015: the African Grey Parrot, the Senegal Parrot, the Monk or Quaker Parrot and the Indian Ringneck Parakeet.Click the PDF link associated with this item to view the complete item as originally published.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: AFA Watchbird; Vol. 49 No. 2 (2022): Watchbird July 2022; 22-23
